title: 1.3 Hash Maps & Sets
summary: Dict/set basics. Hash table internals: hashing, collisions (probing), resizing at 2/3 load, amortized O(1). Two Sum + Contains Duplicate patterns. Group anagrams as homework.
sessionNotes: Excellent conceptual questions about hash table internals; discovered delete-as-you-go gotcha independently; reached solutions with guidance on dict key/value design. Follow-up session: fixed bugs (unhashable set key, variable naming); learned dict values can be containers (grouping pattern).
intro: Dict and set are both built on hash tables — contiguous array, hash function → slot. O(1) average lookup. Collisions handled by probing. Resize at ~2/3 load → amortized O(1) inserts. Rule of thumb: need to RETURN a value (index, count, list) → dict; just need yes/no membership → set. Two Sum: store value → index, look up `target - x`.

body.heading: Dict basics — O(1) average for all core operations
body.content: Hash tables back both dict and set. A hash function maps a key to a slot index. Lookup, insert, delete are all O(1) on average because hashing jumps straight to the slot — no scanning.

body.heading: Dict creation, lookup, .get safe default
body.content:
counts = {}
counts["a"] = 3
counts["b"] = 1

# Safe lookup with .get(key, default)
print(counts.get("a", 0))   # 3
print(counts.get("z", 0))   # 0 — no KeyError

# Iterating
for key in counts:
    print(key, counts[key])

for key, val in counts.items():
    print(key, val)

body.heading: Set basics — like a dict with keys only
body.content:
seen = set()
seen.add(1)
seen.add(2)
seen.add(1)    # duplicate — ignored
print(seen)    # {1, 2}
print(3 in seen)  # False — O(1) check

body.heading: Two Sum — the classic
body.content:
Given a list of numbers and a target, return the indices of two numbers that add up to target.

Key insight: instead of asking "which pair adds up?" ask "have I already seen the number I need?" The dict trades space for time — eliminates the inner loop.

body.heading: Two Sum: brute (O(n²)) vs optimized (O(n))
Contains Duplicate — only need membership
body.content:
If all you care about is "have I seen this before?" — a set is enough. No values needed.

`len(set(nums)) != len(nums)` is the one-liner: if dedup shrinks the collection, there were duplicates.

body.heading: Contains Duplicate: brute vs set

exercises.title: Quiz 1 — Floor division (review)
exercises.critique: Status at time: still partial. Correct phrasing is "floors toward negative infinity," not "rounds down." This item has now reset three times — actively in syntax-drill rotation.

exercises.title: Quiz 2 — join syntax
exercises.critique: Nailed. Correct call on the separator, list passed as the argument.

exercises.title: Quiz 3 — enumerate over range(len(...))
exercises.prompt: Given a list lst, print each index and value using enumerate.
exercises.critique: Only prints `item`, not the index. The prompt asked for both. Also: convention is `i` for index. Otherwise the enumerate usage is correct.

exercises.title: Quiz 4 — Reverse traversal range
exercises.prompt: Write a range() call that goes from index 4 down to 0 (inclusive).
exercises.critique: Correct. start=4 (inclusive), stop=-1 (exclusive, so 0 IS included), step=-1. This is the canonical reverse pattern. Despite getting it here, the item has reset multiple times since.

exercises.title: Quiz 5 — Dict syntax
exercises.prompt: Create an empty dict, add key 'a' with value 1, then check if 'b' is in the dict.
exercises.critique: Correct. Note conventional naming: `d` for a dict variable is the usual choice.

exercises.title: Quiz 6 — Dict as hashmap (is_anagram)
exercises.prompt: In is_anagram: what did the dict key represent, what did the value represent? Why?
exercises.critique: Solid. Captures both directions of the count and why decrementing works. This is exactly the dict-as-counter pattern.

exercises.title: Exercise 1 — first_unique
exercises.prompt: Write first_unique(s): return the index of the first non-repeating character. -1 if none. Brute force first.
exercises.critique: Correct count-then-scan pattern. Pass 1 builds counts; pass 2 returns the first index whose count is 1. The two-pass is necessary because dict iteration order isn't tied to the original string order — you need pass 2 over `s` to preserve input order. Refinement: `hashT[item] = hashT.get(item, 0) + 1` collapses the if/else.

exercises.title: Exercise 2 — intersection
exercises.prompt: Given two lists of integers, return their intersection. Each element unique, order doesn't matter.
exercises.critique: Correct and idiomatic. Both inputs converted to sets first (dedup + O(1) membership). Iterating one set and checking membership in the other gives O(n+m). Pythonic alternative: `list(setA & setB)` — same result, one line.

exercises.title: Exercise 3 — group_anagrams (homework)
exercises.prompt: Group a list of strings by anagram. Hint: what could be a dict key that's the same for all anagrams of the same word?
exercises.critique: Key insight nailed: sorted characters as the dict key. First version maintains a parallel index-tracking variable `x` — works but unnecessarily complex. The second version is the canonical approach: `myDict.setdefault(key, []).append(item)` lets the dict itself hold lists as values, then `list(myDict.values())` gives the groups directly. Dict values can be containers — that's the upgrade. Note from the session: initially tripped on "unhashable set key" (tried using a set as a dict key, which can't be hashable since sets are mutable). Strings are hashable, sorted-then-joined gives a stable, hashable key.

reviewItemsAdded: Hash table mechanics: contiguous memory, hash → slot
O(n+m) when two input sizes differ
Amortized O(1) — occasional resize averages out
Hash table resizing at 2/3 load factor
Two Sum: store `val → index` to return positions
`range()` is lazy, not a stored list
Dict vs set: pick by return value needed
Early exit vs full scan — same Big O, different constants
Delete-as-you-go breaks with odd occurrence counts
Dict values can be containers — grouping pattern
Dict keys must be immutable (hashable) — sets can't be keys
